cls,\n        dtype: type[str],  # also includes `type[str_]`\n        align: builtins.bool = ...,\n        copy: builtins.bool = ...,\n        metadata: dict[str, Any] = ...,\n    ) -> dtype[str_]: ...\n    # NOTE: These `memoryview` overloads assume PEP 688, which requires mypy to\n    # be run with the (undocumented) `--disable-memoryview-promotion` flag,\n    # This will be the default in a operations\n\n    # NOTE: Inplace ops generally use \"same_kind\" casting w.r.t. to the left\n    # operand. An exception to this rule are unsigned integers though, which\n    # also accepts a signed integer for the right operand as long it is a 0D\n    # object and its value is >= 0\n    # NOTE: Due to a ndarray[_ShapeT_co, _DType_co]: ...\n    @overload\n    def __iadd__(self: NDArray[object_], other: Any, /) -> ndarray[_ShapeT_co, _DType_co]: ...\n\n    @overload\n    def __isub__(\n        self: ...\n    @property\n    def nin(self) -> int: ...\n    @property\n    def nout(self) -> int: ...\n    @property\n    def nargs(self) -> int: ...\n    @property\n    def ntypes(self) -> int: ...\n    @property\n    def types(self) -> list[LiteralString]: ...\n    # Broad return type because it has to encompass things like\n    #\n    # >>> np.logical_and.identity is True\n    # True\n    # >>> np.add.identity is 0\n    # True\n    # >>> np.sin.identity is None\n    # True\n    #\n    # and any user-defined ufuncs.\n    @property\n    def identity(self) -> Any: ...\n    # This is None for ufuncs and a string for gufuncs.\n    @property\n    def signature(self) -> None | LiteralString: ...\n\n    def __call__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any: ...\n    # The next four methods will always exist, but they will just\n    # raise a ValueError ufuncs with that don't accept two input\n    # arguments and return one output argument. Because of that we\n    # can't type them very precisely.\n    def reduce(self, /, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any: ...\n    def accumulate(self, /, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> NDArray[Any]: ...\n    def reduceat(self, /, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> NDArray[Any]: ...\n    def outer(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any: ...\n    # Similarly at won't be defined for ufuncs that return multiple\n    # outputs, so we can't type it very precisely.\n    def at(self, /, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None: ...\n\n# Parameters: `__name__`, `ntypes` and `identity`\nabsolute: {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01TWHZB8Q2KYASKygK7VoKsf","type":"tool_result","content":"  5828→\n  5829→def _rolling_median_dispatcher(a, window_size, axis=None, mode=None):\n  5830→    \"\"\"Dispatcher for rolling_median.\"\"\"\n  5831→    return (a,)\n  5832→\n  5833→\n  5834→@array_function_dispatch(_rolling_median_dispatcher)\n  5835→def rolling_median(a, window_size, *, axis=-1, mode='valid'):\n  5836→    \"\"\"\n  5837→    Compute the rolling median of a 1-D or N-D array.\n  5838→\n  5839→    For each position in the array, compute the median of values in a window\n  5840→    of specified size centered (or left-aligned) around that position.\n  5841→\n  5842→    Parameters\n  5843→    ----------\n  5844→    a : array_like\n  5845→        Input array or object that can be converted to an array.\n  5846→    window_size : int\n  5847→        Size of the rolling window. Must be a positive integer.\n  5848→    axis : int, optional\n  5849→        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n  5850→    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n  5851→        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n  5852→\n  5853→        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n  5854→          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n  5855→        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n  5856→          output is the same size as the larger input.\n  5857→        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n  5858→          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n  5859→\n  5860→    Returns\n  5861→    -------\n  5862→    rolling_med : ndarray\n  5863→        The rolling median of the input array. For\n  5880→    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n  5881→    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n  5882→    array is padded on both sides.\n  5883→\n  5884→    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n  5885→    each window.\n  5886→    \"\"\"\n  5887→    a = asanyarray(a)\n  5888→\n  5889→    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n  5890→        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n  5891→\n  5892→    window_size = int(window_size)\n  5893→    if window_size <= 0:\n  5894→        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n  5895→\n  5896→    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n  5897→        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n  5898→\n  5899→    # Normalize axis\n  5900→    if axis is None:\n  5901→        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n  5902→\n  5903→    ndim = a.ndim\n  5904→    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n  5905→\n  5906→    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n  5907→        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - Must be a positive integer.\n    axis : int, optional\n        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n\n        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n          output is the same size as the larger input.\n        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    rolling_med : ndarray\n        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n        parameter and the input shape.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n    array([2., 3., 4.])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n    array is padded on both sides.\n\n    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n    each window.\n    \"\"\"\n    a = asanyarray(a)\n\n    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n\n    window_size = int(window_size)\n    if window_size <= 0:\n        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n\n    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n\n    # Normalize axis\n    if axis is None:\n        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n\n    ndim = a.ndim\n    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n\n    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - ndim if axis >= 0 else axis} is out of bounds for array of dimension {ndim}\")\n\n    # Get the size along the specified axis\n    axis_size = a.shape[axis]\n\n    # Handle edge cases\n    if window_size > axis_size:\n        # Return NaN for entire output if window is larger than array\n        if mode == 'valid':\n            # No valid output\n            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n            new_shape[axis] = 0\n            return empty(tuple(new_shape), dtype=np.float64)\n        elif mode == 'same':\n            return np.full(a.shape, np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n        else:  # mode == 'full'\n            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n            new_shape[axis] = axis_size + window_size - 1\n            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n\n    # Compute padding","numLines":100,"startLine":5828,"totalLines":5975}}}

Pass each agent the full diff so it has the complete context.\n\n### Agent 1: Code Reuse Review\n\nFor each change:\n\n1. **Search for existing utilities and helpers** that could replace newly written code. handling, custom environment checks, ad-hoc type guards, and similar patterns are common candidates.\n\n### Agent 2: Code Quality Review\n\nReview the same changes for hacky patterns:\n\n1. **Redundant state**: state that duplicates existing state, cached values that could be derived, observers/effects that could be direct calls\n2. **Parameter sprawl**: adding new parameters to a function instead of generalizing or restructuring existing ones\n3. **Copy-paste with slight variation**: near-duplicate code blocks that should be unified with a shared abstraction\n4. **Leaky abstractions**: exposing internal details that should be encapsulated, or breaking existing abstraction boundaries\n5. **Stringly-typed code**: using raw strings where constants, enums (string unions), or branded types already exist in the codebase\n\n### Agent 3: Efficiency Review\n\nReview the same changes for efficiency:\n\n1. **Unnecessary work**: redundant computations, repeated file reads, duplicate network/API calls, N+1 patterns\n2. **Missed concurrency**: independent operations run sequentially when they could run in parallel\n3. **Hot-path bloat**: new blocking work added to startup or per-request/per-render hot paths\n4. **Unnecessary existence checks**: pre-checking file/resource existence before operating (TOCTOU anti-pattern) — operate directly and handle the error\n5. **Memory**: unbounded data structures, missing cleanup, event listener leaks\n6. **Overly broad operations**: reading entire files when only a portion is needed, loading all items when filtering for one\n\n## Phase 3: Fix Issues\n\nWait for all three agents to complete. Aggregate their findings and fix each issue directly. If a finding is a false positive or not worth addressing, note it and move on — do not argue with the finding, just skip it.\n\nWhen done, briefly summarize what was fixed (or confirm the code was already clean).\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"d4cb46fc-49fd-49ab-9b24-1539e74def50","uuid":"22a7e497-0e05-49ab-8c57-156b90a55468","isSynthetic":true}

Must be a positive integer.\n+    axis : int, optional\n+        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n+    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n+        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n+\n+        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n+          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n+        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n+          output is the same size as the larger input.\n+        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n+          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n+\n+    Returns\n+    -------\n+    rolling_med : ndarray\n+        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n+        parameter and the input shape.\n+\n+    Examples\n+    --------\n+    >>> import numpy as np\n+    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n+    array([2., 3., 4.])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n+    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n+    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n+\n+    Notes\n+    -----\n+    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n+    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n+    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n+    array is padded on both sides.\n+\n+    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n+    each window.\n+    \"\"\"\n+    a = asanyarray(a)\n+\n+    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n+        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n+\n+    window_size = int(window_size)\n+    if window_size <= 0:\n+        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n+\n+    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n+        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n+\n+    # Normalize axis\n+    if axis is None:\n+        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n+\n+    ndim = a.ndim\n+    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n+\n+    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n+        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - ndim if axis >= 0 else axis} is out of bounds for array of dimension {ndim}\")\n+\n+    # Get the size along the specified axis\n+    axis_size = a.shape[axis]\n+\n+    # Handle edge cases\n+    if window_size > axis_size:\n+        # Return NaN for entire output if window is larger than array\n+        if mode == 'valid':\n+            # No valid output\n+            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n+            new_shape[axis] = 0\n+            return empty(tuple(new_shape), dtype=np.float64)\n+        elif mode == 'same':\n+            return np.full(a.shape, np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n+        else:  # mode == 'full'\n+            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n+            new_shape[axis] = axis_size + window_size - 1\n+            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n+\n+    # Compute padding\n+    if mode == 'valid':\n+        pad_before = 0\n+        pad_after = 0\n+        output_size = axis_size - window_size + 1\n+    elif mode == 'same':\n+        # Pad to maintain same size\n+        total_pad = window_size - 1\n+        pad_before = total_pad // 2\n+        pad_after = total_pad - pad_before\n+        output_size = axis_size\n+    else:  # mode == 'full'\n+        pad_before = window_size - 1\n+        pad_after = window_size - 1\n+        output_size = axis_size + window_size - 1\n+\n+    # Apply padding\n+    padded_shape = list(a.shape)\n+    padded_shape[axis] = axis_size + pad_before + pad_after\n+    padded = np.pad(a, [(pad_before, pad_after) if i == axis else (0, 0)\n+                        for i in range(ndim)], mode='edge')\n+\n+    # Compute rolling median using a loop over windows\n+    # Move axis to position -1 for easier iteration\n+    a_moved = np.moveaxis(padded, axis, -1)\n+    original_shape = a_moved.shape\n+\n+    # Reshape to (batch, length) for easier processing\n+    batch_shape = original_shape[:-1]\n+    length = original_shape[-1]\n+    a_flat = a_moved.reshape(-1, length)\n+\n+    # Initialize output\n+    output_flat = np.zeros((a_flat.shape[0], output_size), dtype=np.float64)\n+\n+    # Compute rolling median for each batch element\n+    for i in range(output_size):\n+        window = a_flat[:, i:i + window_size]\n+        output_flat[:, i] = np.median(window, axis=1)\n+\n+    # Reshape back\n+    output_shape = list(batch_shape) + [output_size]\n+    output = output_flat.reshape(output_shape)\n+\n+    # Move axis back to original position\n+    output = np.moveaxis(output, -1, axis)\n+\n+ Must be a positive integer.\n+    axis : int, optional\n+        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n+    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n+        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n+\n+        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n+          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n+        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n+          output is the same size as the larger input.\n+        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n+          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n+\n+    Returns\n+    -------\n+    rolling_med : ndarray\n+        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n+        parameter and the input shape.\n+\n+    Examples\n+    --------\n+    >>> import numpy as np\n+    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n+    array([2., 3., 4.])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n+    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n+    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n+\n+    Notes\n+    -----\n+    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n+    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n+    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n+    array is padded on both sides.\n+\n+    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n+    each window.\n+    \"\"\"\n+    a = asanyarray(a)\n+\n+ pad_before\n+        output_size = axis_size\n+    else:  # mode == 'full'\n+        pad_before = window_size - 1\n+        pad_after = window_size - 1\n+        output_size = axis_size + window_size original_shape[-1]\n+    a_flat = a_moved.reshape(-1, length)\n+\n+    # Initialize output\n+    output_flat = np.zeros((a_flat.shape[0], output_size), dtype=np.float64)\n+\n+    # Compute rolling median for each batch element\n+    for i in range(output_size):\n+        window = a_flat[:, i:i + window_size]\n+        output_flat[:, i] = np.median(window, axis=1)\n+\n+    # Reshape back\n+ Must be a positive integer.\n  5848→    axis : int, optional\n  5849→        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n  5850→    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n  5851→        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n  5852→\n  5853→        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n  5854→          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n  5855→        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n  5856→          output is the same size as the larger input.\n  5857→        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n  5858→          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n  5859→\n  5860→    Returns\n  5861→    -------\n  5862→    rolling_med : ndarray\n  5863→        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n  5864→        parameter and the input shape.\n  5865→\n  5866→    Examples\n  5867→    --------\n  5868→    >>> import numpy as np\n  5869→    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n  5870→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n  5871→    array([2., 3., 4.])\n  5872→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n  5873→    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n  5874→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n  5875→    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n  5876→\n  5877→    Notes\n  5878→    -----\n  5879→    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n  5880→    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n  5881→    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n  5882→    array is padded on both sides.\n  5883→\n  5884→    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n  5885→    each window.\n  5886→    \"\"\"\n  5887→    a = asanyarray(a)\n  5888→\n  5889→    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n  5890→        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n  5891→\n  5892→    window_size = int(window_size)\n  5893→    if window_size <= 0:\n  5894→        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n  5895→\n  5896→    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n  5897→        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n  5898→\n  5899→    # Normalize axis\n  5900→    if axis is None:\n  5901→        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n  5902→\n  5903→    ndim = a.ndim\n  5904→    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n  5905→\n  5906→    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n  5907→        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - ndim if axis >= 0 else axis} is out of bounds for array of dimension {ndim}\")\n  5908→\n  5909→    # Get the size along the specified axis\n  5910→    axis_size = a.shape[axis]\n  5911→\n  5912→    # Handle edge cases\n  5913→    if window_size > axis_size:\n  5914→        # Return NaN for entire output if window is larger than array\n  5915→        if mode == 'valid':\n  5916→            # No valid output\n  5917→            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n  5918→            new_shape[axis] = 0\n  5919→            return empty(tuple(new_shape), dtype=np.float64)\n  5920→        elif mode == 'same':\n  5921→            return np.full(a.shape, np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n  5922→        else:  # mode == 'full'\n  5923→            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n  5924→            new_shape[axis] = axis_size + window_size - 1\n  5925→            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n  5926→\n  5927→    # Compute padding\n  5928→    if mode == 'valid':\n  5929→        pad_before = 0\n  5930→        pad_after = 0\n  5931→        output_size = axis_size - window_size + 1\n  5932→    elif mode == 'same':\n  5933→        # Pad to maintain same size\n  5934→        total_pad = window_size - 1\n  5935→        pad_before = total_pad // 2\n  5936→        pad_after = total_pad - pad_before\n  5937→        output_size = axis_size\n  5938→    else:  # mode == 'full'\n  5939→        pad_before = window_size - 1\n  5940→        pad_after = window_size - 1\n  5941→        output_size = axis_size + window_size - 1\n  5942→\n  5943→    # Apply padding\n  5944→    padded_shape = list(a.shape)\n  5945→    padded_shape[axis] = axis_size + pad_before + pad_after\n  5946→    padded = np.pad(a, [(pad_before, pad_after) if i == axis else (0, 0)\n  5947→                        for i in range(ndim)], mode='edge')\n  5948→\n  5949→    # Compute rolling median using a loop over windows\n  5950→    # Move axis to position -1 for easier iteration\n  5951→    a_moved = np.moveaxis(padded, axis, -1)\n  5952→    original_shape = a_moved.shape\n  5953→\n  5954→    # Reshape to (batch, length) for easier processing\n  5955→    batch_shape = original_shape[:-1]\n  5956→    length = original_shape[-1]\n  5957→    a_flat = a_moved.reshape(-1, length)\n  5958→\n  5959→    # Initialize output\n  5960→    output_flat = np.zeros((a_flat.shape[0], output_size), dtype=np.float64)\n  5961→\n  5962→    # Compute rolling median for each batch element\n  5963→    for i in range(output_size):\n  5964→        window = a_flat[:, i:i + window_size]\n  5965→        output_flat[:, i] = np.median(window, axis=1)\n  5966→\n  5967→    # Reshape back\n  5968→    output_shape = list(batch_shape) + [output_size]\n  5969→    output = output_flat.reshape(output_shape)\n  5970→\n  5971→    # Move axis back to original position\n  5972→    output = np.moveaxis(output, -1, axis)\n  5973→\n  5974→    return output\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01LMHqgRZ8kbxf39W5ncu8NS","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→\"\"\"\n     2→Utilities that manipulate strides to achieve desirable effects.\n     3→\n     4→An explanation of strides can be found in the :ref:`arrays.ndarray`.\n     5→\n     6→\"\"\"\n     7→import numpy as np\n     8→from numpy._core.numeric import normalize_axis_tuple\n     9→from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch, set_module\n    10→\n    11→__all__ = ['broadcast_to', 'broadcast_arrays', 'broadcast_shapes']\n    12→\n    13→\n    14→class DummyArray:\n    15→    \"\"\"Dummy object that just exists to hang __array_interface__ dictionaries\n    16→    and possibly keep alive a reference to a base array.\n    17→    \"\"\"\n    18→\n    19→    def __init__(self, interface, base=None):\n    20→        self.__array_interface__ = interface\n    21→        self.base = base\n    22→\n    23→\n    24→def _maybe_view_as_subclass(original_array, new_array):\n    25→    if type(original_array) is not type(new_array):\n    26→        # if input was an ndarray subclass and subclasses were OK,\n    27→        # then view the result as that subclass.\n    28→        new_array = new_array.view(type=type(original_array))\n    29→        # Since we have done something akin to a view from original_array, we\n    30→        # should let the subclass finalize (if it has it implemented, i.e., is\n    31→        # not None).\n    32→        if new_array.__array_finalize__:\n    33→            new_array.__array_finalize__(original_array)\n    34→    return new_array\n    35→\n    36→\n    37→@set_module(\"numpy.lib.stride_tricks\")\n    38→def as_strided(x, shape=None, strides=None, subok=False, writeable=True):\n    39→    \"\"\"\n    40→    Create a view into the array with the given shape and strides.\n    41→\n    42→    .. warning:: This function has to be used with extreme care, see notes.\n    43→\n    44→    Parameters\n    45→    ----------\n    46→    x : ndarray\n    47→        Array to create a new.\n    48→    shape : sequence of int, optional\n    49→        The shape of the new array. Defaults to ``x.shape``.\n    50→    strides : sequence of int, optional\n    51→        The strides of the new array. Defaults to ``x.strides``.\n    52→    subok : bool, optional\n    53→        If True, subclasses are preserved.\n    54→    writeable : bool, optional\n    55→        If set to False, the returned array will always be readonly.\n    56→        Otherwise it will be writable if the original array was. It\n    57→        is advisable to set this to False if possible (see Notes).\n    58→\n    59→    Returns\n    60→    -------\n    61→    view : ndarray\n    62→\n    63→    See also\n    64→    --------\n    65→    broadcast_to : broadcast an array to a given shape.\n    66→    reshape : reshape an array.\n    67→    lib.stride_tricks.sliding_window_view :\n    68→        userfriendly and safe function for a creation of sliding window views.\n    69→\n    70→    Notes\n    71→    -----\n    72→    ``as_strided`` creates a view into the array given the exact strides\n    73→    and shape. This means it manipulates the internal data structure of\n    74→    ndarray and, if done incorrectly, the array elements can point to\n    75→    invalid memory and can corrupt results or crash your program.\n    76→    It is advisable to always use the original ``x.strides`` when\n    77→    calculating new strides to avoid reliance on a contiguous memory\n    78→    layout.\n    79→\n    80→    Furthermore, arrays created with this function often contain self\n    81→    overlapping memory, so that two elements are identical.\n    82→    Vectorized write operations on such arrays will typically be\n    83→    unpredictable. They may even give different results for small, large,\n    84→    or transposed arrays.\n    85→\n    86→    Since writing to these arrays has to be tested and done with great\n    87→    care, you may want to use ``writeable=False`` to avoid accidental write\n    88→    operations.\n    89→\n    90→    For these reasons it is advisable to avoid ``as_strided`` when\n    91→    possible.\n    92→    \"\"\"\n    93→    # first convert input to array, possibly keeping subclass\n    94→    x = np.array(x, copy=None, subok=subok)\n    95→    interface = dict(x.__array_interface__)\n    96→    if shape is not None:\n    97→        interface['shape'] = tuple(shape)\n    98→    if strides is not None:\n    99→        interface['strides'] = tuple(strides)\n   100→\n   101→    array = np.asarray(DummyArray(interface, base=x))\n   102→    # The route via `__interface__` does not preserve structured\n   103→    # dtypes. Single integers `i` are treated as if they were the\n   141→        tuple `(i,)`.\n   142→    axis : int or tuple of int, optional\n   143→        Axis or axes along which the sliding window is applied.\n   144→        By default, the sliding window is applied to all axes and\n   145→        `window_shape[i]` will refer to axis `i` of `x`.\n   146→        If `axis` is given as a `tuple of int`, `window_shape[i]` will refer to\n   147→        the axis `axis[i]` of `x`.\n   148→        Single integers `i` are treated as if they were the tuple `(i,)`.\n   149→    subok : bool, optional\n   150→        If True, sub-classes will be passed-through, otherwise the returned\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01QycsXi2hqh4EKjP7PWx8Rz","type":"tool_result","content":"   693→def correlate(a, v, mode='valid'):\n   694→    r\"\"\"\n   695→    Cross-correlation of two 1-dimensional sequences.\n   696→\n   697→    This function computes the correlation as generally defined in signal\n   698→    processing texts [1]_:\n   699→\n   700→    .. math:: c_k = \\sum_n a_{n+k} \\cdot \\overline{v}_n\n   701→\n   702→    with a and v sequences being zero-padded where necessary and\n   703→    :math:`\\overline v` denoting complex conjugation.\n   704→\n   705→    Parameters\n   706→    ----------\n   707→    a, v : array_like\n   708→        Input sequences.\n   709→    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n   710→        Refer to the `convolve` docstring.  Note that the default\n   711→        is 'valid', unlike `convolve`, which uses 'full'.\n   712→\n   713→    Returns\n   714→    -------\n   715→    out : ndarray\n   716→        Discrete cross-correlation of `a` and `v`.\n   717→\n   718→    See Also\n   719→    --------\n   720→    convolve : Discrete, linear convolution of two one-dimensional sequences.\n   721→    scipy.signal.correlate : uses FFT which has superior performance\n   722→        on large arrays.\n   723→\n   724→    Notes\n   725→    -----\n   726→    The definition of correlation above is not unique and sometimes\n   727→    correlation may be defined differently. Another common definition is [1]_:\n   728→\n   729→    .. math:: c'_k = \\sum_n a_{n} \\cdot \\overline{v_{n+k}}\n   730→\n   731→    which is related to :math:`c_k` by :math:`c'_k = c_{-k}`.\n   732→\n   733→    `numpy.correlate` may perform slowly in large arrays (i.e. n = 1e5)\n   734→    because it does not use the FFT to compute the convolution; in that case,\n   735→    `scipy.signal.correlate` might be preferable.\n   736→\n   737→    References\n   738→    ----------\n   739→    .. [1] Wikipedia, \"Cross-correlation\",\n   740→           https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-correlation\n   741→\n   742→    Examples\n   743→    --------\n   744→    >>> import numpy as np\n   745→    >>> np.correlate([1, 2, 3], [0, 1, 0.5])\n   746→    array([3.5])\n   747→    >>> np.correlate([1, 2, 3], [0, 1, 0.5], \"same\")\n   748→    array([2. ,  3.5,  3. ])\n   749→    >>> np.correlate([1, 2, 3], [0, 1, 0.5], \"full\")\n   750→    array([0.5,  2. ,  3.5,  3. ,  0. ])\n   751→\n   752→    Using complex sequences:\n   753→\n   754→    >>> np.correlate([1+1j, 2, 3-1j], [0, 1, 0.5j], 'full')\n   755→    array([ 0.5-0.5j,  1.0+0.j ,  1.5-1.5j,  3.0-1.j ,  0.0+0.j ])\n   756→\n   757→    Note that you get the time reversed, complex conjugated result\n   758→    (:math:`\\overline{c_{-k}}`) when the two input sequences a and v change\n   759→    places:\n   760→\n   761→    >>> np.correlate([0, 1, 0.5j], [1+1j, 2, 3-1j], 'full')\n   762→    array([ 0.0+0.j ,  3.0+1.j ,  1.5+1.5j,  1.0+0.j ,  0.5+0.5j])\n   763→\n   764→    \"\"\"\n   765→    return multiarray.correlate2(a, v, mode)\n   766→\n   767→\n   768→def _convolve_dispatcher(a, v, mode=None):\n   769→    return (a, v)\n   770→\n   771→\n   772→@array_function_dispatch(_convolve_dispatcher)\n   773→def convolve(a, v, mode='full'):\n   774→    \"\"\"\n   775→    Returns the discrete, linear convolution of two one-dimensional sequences.\n   776→\n   777→    The convolution operator is often seen in signal processing, where it\n   778→    models the effect of a linear time-invariant system on a signal [1]_.  In\n   779→    probability theory, the sum of two independent random variables is\n   780→    distributed according to the convolution of their individual\n   781→    distributions.\n   782→\n   783→    If `v` is longer than `a`, the arrays are swapped before computation.\n   784→\n   785→    Parameters\n   786→    ----------\n   787→    a : (N,) array_like\n   788→        First one-dimensional input array.\n   789→    v : (M,) array_like\n   790→        Second one-dimensional input array.\n   791→    mode : {'full', 'valid', 'same'}, optional\n   792→        'full':\n   793→          By default, mode is 'full'.  This returns the convolution\n   794→          at each point of overlap, with an output shape of (N+M-1,). At\n   795→          the end-points of the convolution, the signals do not overlap\n   796→          completely, and boundary effects may be seen.\n   797→\n   798→        'same':\n   799→          Mode 'same' returns output of length ``max(M, N)``.  Boundary\n   800→          effects are still visible.\n   801→\n   802→        'valid':\n   803→          Mode 'valid' returns output of length\n   804→          ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.  The convolution product is only given\n   805→          for points where the signals overlap completely.  Values outside\n   806→          the signal boundary have no effect.\n   807→\n   808→    Returns\n   809→    -------\n   810→    out : ndarray\n   811→        Discrete, linear convolution of `a` and `v`.\n   812→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01NdGfnSg3jQNHZCU8LZopuU","type":"tool_result","content":"   773→def convolve(a, v, mode='full'):\n   774→    \"\"\"\n   775→    Returns the discrete, linear convolution of two one-dimensional sequences.\n   776→\n   777→    The convolution operator is often seen in signal processing, where it\n   778→    models the effect of a linear time-invariant system on a signal [1]_.  In\n   779→    probability theory, the sum of two independent random variables is\n   780→    distributed according to the convolution of their individual\n   781→    distributions.\n   782→\n   783→    If `v` is longer than `a`, the arrays are swapped before computation.\n   784→\n   785→    Parameters\n   786→    ----------\n   787→    a : (N,) array_like\n   788→        First one-dimensional input array.\n   789→    v : (M,) array_like\n   790→        Second one-dimensional input array.\n   791→    mode : {'full', 'valid', 'same'}, optional\n   792→        'full':\n   793→          By default, mode is 'full'.  This returns the convolution\n   794→          at each point of overlap, with an output shape of (N+M-1,). At\n   795→          the end-points of the convolution, the signals do not overlap\n   796→          completely, and boundary effects may be seen.\n   797→\n   798→        'same':\n   799→          Mode 'same' returns output of length ``max(M, N)``.  Boundary\n   800→          effects are still visible.\n   801→\n   802→        'valid':\n   803→          Mode 'valid' returns output of length\n   804→          ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.  The convolution product is only given\n   805→          for points where the signals overlap completely.  Values outside\n   806→          the signal boundary have no effect.\n   807→\n   808→    Returns\n   809→    -------\n   810→    out : ndarray\n   811→        Discrete, linear convolution of `a` and `v`.\n   812→\n   813→    See Also\n   814→    --------\n   815→    scipy.signal.fftconvolve : Convolve two arrays using the Fast Fourier\n   816→                               Transform.\n   817→    scipy.linalg.toeplitz : Used to construct the convolution operator.\n   818→    polymul : Polynomial multiplication. Same output as convolve, but also\n   819→              accepts poly1d objects as input.\n   820→\n   821→    Notes\n   822→    -----\n   823→    The discrete convolution operation is defined as\n   824→\n   825→    .. math:: (a * v)_n = \\\\sum_{m = -\\\\infty}^{\\\\infty} a_m v_{n - m}\n   826→\n   827→    It can be shown that a convolution :math:`x(t) * y(t)` in time/space\n   828→    is equivalent to the multiplication :math:`X(f) Y(f)` in the Fourier\n   829→    domain, after appropriate padding (padding is necessary to prevent\n   830→    circular convolution).  Since multiplication is more efficient (faster)\n   831→    than convolution, the function `scipy.signal.fftconvolve` exploits the\n   832→    FFT to calculate the convolution of large data-sets.\n   833→\n   834→    References\n   835→    ----------\n   836→    .. [1] Wikipedia, \"Convolution\",\n   837→        https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution\n   838→\n   839→    Examples\n   840→    --------\n   841→    Note how the convolution operator flips the second array\n   842→    before \"sliding\" the two across one another:\n   843→\n   844→    >>> import numpy as np\n   845→    >>> np.convolve([1, 2, 3], [0, 1, 0.5])\n   846→    array([0. , 1. , 2.5, 4. , 1.5])\n   847→\n   848→    Only return the middle values of the convolution.\n   849→    Contains boundary effects, where zeros are taken\n   850→    into account:\n   851→\n   852→    >>> np.convolve([1,2,3],[0,1,0.5], 'same')\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_012Uba8pnPS6u3cE9g2T36x1","type":"tool_result","content":"   122→def sliding_window_view(x, window_shape, axis=None, *,\n   123→                        subok=False, writeable=False):\n   124→    \"\"\"\n   125→    Create a sliding window view into the array with the given window shape.\n   126→\n   127→    Also known as rolling or moving window, the window slides across all\n   128→    dimensions of the array and extracts subsets of the array at all window\n   129→    positions.\n   130→\n   131→    .. versionadded:: 1.20.0\n   132→\n   133→    Parameters\n   134→    ----------\n   135→    x : array_like\n   136→        Array to create the sliding window view from.\n   137→    window_shape : int or tuple of int\n   138→        Size of window over each axis that takes part in the sliding window.\n   139→        If `axis` is not present, must have same length as the number of input\n   140→        array dimensions. Single integers `i` are treated as if they were the\n   141→        tuple `(i,)`.\n   142→    axis : int or tuple of int, optional\n   143→        Axis or axes along which the sliding window is applied.\n   144→        By default, the sliding window is applied to all axes and\n   145→        `window_shape[i]` will refer to axis `i` of `x`.\n   146→        If `axis` is given as a `tuple of int`, `window_shape[i]` will refer to\n   147→        the axis `axis[i]` of `x`.\n   148→        Single integers `i` are treated as if they were the tuple `(i,)`.\n   149→    subok : bool, optional\n   150→        If True, sub-classes will be passed-through, otherwise the returned\n   151→        array will be forced to be a base-class array (default).\n   152→    writeable : bool, optional\n   153→        When true, allow writing to the returned view. The default is false,\n   154→        as this should be used with caution: the returned view contains the\n   155→        same memory location multiple times, so writing to one location will\n   156→        cause others to change.\n   157→\n   158→    Returns\n   159→    -------\n   160→    view : ndarray\n   161→        Sliding window view of the array. The sliding window dimensions are\n   162→        inserted at the end, and the original dimensions are trimmed as\n   163→        required by the size of the sliding window.\n   164→        That is, ``view.shape = x_shape_trimmed + window_shape``, where\n   165→        ``x_shape_trimmed`` is ``x.shape`` with every entry reduced by one less\n   166→        than the corresponding window size.\n   167→\n   168→    See Also\n   169→    --------\n   170→    lib.stride_tricks.as_strided: A lower-level and less safe routine for\n   171→        creating arbitrary views from custom shape and strides.\n   172→    broadcast_to: broadcast an array to a given shape.\n   173→\n   174→    Notes\n   175→    -----\n   176→    For many applications using a sliding window view can be convenient, but\n   177→    potentially very slow. Often specialized solutions exist, for example:\n   178→\n   179→    - `scipy.signal.fftconvolve`\n   180→\n   181→    - filtering functions in `scipy.ndimage`\n   182→\n   183→    - moving window functions provided by\n   184→      `bottleneck <https://github.com/pydata/bottleneck>`_.\n   185→\n   186→    As a rough estimate, a sliding window approach with an input size of `N`\n   187→    and a window size of `W` will scale as `O(N*W)` where frequently a special\n   188→    algorithm can achieve `O(N)`. That means that the sliding window variant\n   189→    for a window size of 100 can be a 100 times slower than a more specialized\n   190→    version.\n   191→\n   192→    Nevertheless, for small window sizes, when no custom algorithm exists, or\n   193→    as a prototyping and developing tool, this function can be a good solution.\n   194→\n   195→    Examples\n   196→    --------\n   197→    >>> import numpy as np\n   198→    >>> from numpy.lib.stride_tricks import sliding_window_view\n   199→    >>> x = np.arange(6)\n   200→    >>> x.shape\n   201→    (6,)\n   202→    >>> v = sliding_window_view(x, 3)\n   203→    >>> v.shape\n   204→    (4, 3)\n   205→    >>> v\n   206→    array([[0, 1, 2],\n   207→           [1, 2, 3],\n   208→           [2, 3, 4],\n   209→           [3, 4, 5]])\n   210→\n   211→    This also works in more dimensions, e.g.\n   212→\n   213→    >>> i, j = np.ogrid[:3, :4]\n   214→    >>> x = 10*i + j\n   215→    >>> x.shape\n   216→    (3, 4)\n   217→    >>> x\n   218→    array([[ 0,  1,  2,  3],\n   219→           [10, 11, 12, 13],\n   220→           [20, 21, 22, 23]])\n   221→    >>> shape = (2,2)\n   222→    >>> v = sliding_window_view(x, shape)\n   223→    >>> v.shape\n   224→    (2, 3, 2, 2)\n   225→    >>> v\n   226→    array([[[[ 0,  1],\n   227→             [10, 11]],\n   228→            [[ 1,  2],\n   229→             [11, 12]],\n   230→            [[ 2,  3],\n   231→             [12, 13]]],\n   232→           [[[10, 11],\n   233→             [20, 21]],\n   234→            [[11, 12],\n   235→             [21, 22]],\n   236→            [[12, 13],\n   237→             [22, 23]]]])\n   238→\n   239→    The axis can be specified explicitly:\n   240→\n   241→    >>> v = sliding_window_view(x, 3, 0)\n   242→    >>> v.shape\n   243→    (1, 4, 3)\n   244→    >>> v\n   245→    array([[[ 0, 10, 20],\n   246→            [ 1, 11, 21],\n   247→            [ 2, 12, 22],\n   248→            [ 3, 13, 23]]])\n   249→\n   250→    The same axis can be used several times. In that case, every use reduces\n   251→    the corresponding original dimension:\n   252→\n   253→    >>> v = sliding_window_view(x, (2, 3), (1, 1))\n   254→    >>> v.shape\n   255→    (3, 1, 2, 3)\n   256→    >>> v\n   257→    array([[[[ 0,  1,  2],\n   258→             [ 1,  2,  3]]],\n   259→           [[[10, 11, 12],\n   260→             [11, 12, 13]]],\n   261→           [[[20, 21, 22],\n   262→             [21, 22, 23]]]])\n   263→\n   264→    Combining with stepped slicing (`::step`), this can be used to take sliding\n   265→    views which skip elements:\n   266→\n   267→    >>> x = np.arange(7)\n   268→    >>> sliding_window_view(x, 5)[:, ::2]\n   269→    array([[0, 2, 4],\n   270→           [1, 3, 5],\n   271→           [2, 4, 6]])\n   272→\n   273→    or views which move by multiple elements\n   274→\n   275→    >>> x = np.arange(7)\n   276→    >>> sliding_window_view(x, 3)[::2, :]\n   277→    array([[0, 1, 2],\n   278→           [2, 3, 4],\n   279→           [4, 5, 6]])\n   280→\n   281→    A common application of `sliding_window_view` is the calculation of running\n   282→    statistics. The simplest example is the\n   283→    `moving average <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_average>`_:\n   284→\n   285→    >>> x = np.arange(6)\n   286→    >>> x.shape\n   287→    (6,)\n   288→    >>> v = sliding_window_view(x, 3)\n   289→    >>> v.shape\n   290→    (4, 3)\n   291→    >>> v\n   292→    array([[0, 1, 2],\n   293→           [1, 2, 3],\n   294→           [2, 3, 4],\n   295→           [3, 4, 5]])\n   296→    >>> moving_average = v.mean(axis=-1)\n   297→    >>> moving_average\n   298→    array([1., 2., 3., 4.])\n   299→\n   300→    Note that a sliding window approach is often **not** optimal (see Notes).\n   301→    \"\"\"\n   302→    window_shape = (tuple(window_shape)\n   303→                    if np.iterable(window_shape)\n   304→                    else (window_shape,))\n   305→    # first convert input to array, possibly keeping subclass\n   306→    x = np.array(x, copy=None, subok=subok)\n   307→\n   308→    window_shape_array = np.array(window_shape)\n   309→    if np.any(window_shape_array < 0):\n   310→        raise ValueError('`window_shape` cannot contain negative values')\n   311→\n   312→    if axis is None:\n   313→        axis = tuple(range(x.ndim))\n   314→        if len(window_shape) != len(axis):\n   315→            raise ValueError(f'Since axis is `None`, must provide '\n   316→                             f'window_shape for all dimensions of `x`; '\n   317→                             f'got {len(window_shape)} window_shape elements '\n   318→                             f'and `x.ndim` is {x.ndim}.')\n   319→    else:\n   320→        axis = normalize_axis_tuple(axis, x.ndim, allow_duplicate=True)\n   321→        if len(window_shape) != len(axis):\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01LNKB5SVvHC4uoL3uMu621S","type":"tool_result","content":"  4068→def percentile(a,\n  4069→               q,\n  4070→               axis=None,\n  4071→               out=None,\n  4072→               overwrite_input=False,\n  4073→               method=\"linear\",\n  4074→               keepdims=False,\n  4075→               *,\n  4076→               weights=None,\n  4077→               interpolation=None):\n  4078→    \"\"\"\n  4079→    Compute the q-th percentile of the data along the specified axis.\n  4080→\n  4081→    Returns the q-th percentile(s) of the array elements.\n  4082→\n  4083→    Parameters\n  4084→    ----------\n  4085→    a : array_like of real numbers\n  4086→        Input array or object that can be converted to an array.\n  4087→    q : array_like of float\n  4088→        Percentage or sequence of percentages for the percentiles to compute.\n  4089→        Values must be between 0 and 100 inclusive.\n  4090→    axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional\n  4091→        Axis or axes along which the percentiles are computed. The\n  4092→        default is to compute the percentile(s) along a flattened\n  4093→        version of the array.\n  4094→    out : ndarray, optional\n  4095→        Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must\n  4096→        have the same shape and buffer length as the expected output,\n  4097→        but the type (of the output) will be cast if necessary.\n  4098→    overwrite_input : bool, optional\n  4099→        If True, then allow the input array `a` to be modified by intermediate\n  4100→        calculations, to save memory. In this case, the contents of the input\n  4101→        `a` after this function completes is undefined.\n  4102→    method : str, optional\n  4103→        This parameter specifies the method to use for estimating the\n  4104→        percentile.  There are many different methods, some unique to NumPy.\n  4105→        See the notes for explanation.  The options sorted by their R type\n  4106→        as summarized in the H&F paper [1]_ are:\n  4107→\n  4108→        1. 'inverted_cdf'\n  4109→        2. 'averaged_inverted_cdf'\n  4110→        3. 'closest_observation'\n  4111→        4. 'interpolated_inverted_cdf'\n  4112→        5. 'hazen'\n  4113→        6. 'weibull'\n  4114→        7. 'linear'  (default)\n  4115→        8. 'median_unbiased'\n  4116→        9. 'normal_unbiased'\n  4117→\n  4118→        The first three methods are discontinuous.  NumPy further defines the\n  4119→        following discontinuous variations of the default 'linear' (7.) option:\n  4120→\n  4121→        * 'lower'\n  4122→        * 'higher',\n  4123→        * 'midpoint'\n  4124→        * 'nearest'\n  4125→\n  4126→        .. versionchanged:: 1.22.0\n  4127→            This argument was previously called \"interpolation\" and only\n  4128→            offered the \"linear\" default and last four options.\n  4129→\n  4130→    keepdims : bool, optional\n  4131→        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left in\n  4132→        the result as dimensions with size one. With this option, the\n  4133→        result will broadcast correctly against the original array `a`.\n  4134→\n  4135→     weights : array_like, optional\n  4136→        An array of weights associated with the values in `a`. Each value in\n  4137→        `a` contributes to the percentile according to its associated weight.\n  4138→        The weights array can either be 1-D (in which case its length must be\n  4139→        the size of `a` along the given axis) or of the same shape as `a`.\n  4140→        If `weights=None`, then all data in `a` are assumed to have a\n  4141→        weight equal to one.\n  4142→        Only `method=\"inverted_cdf\"` supports weights.\n  4143→        See the notes for more details.\n  4144→\n  4145→        .. versionadded:: 2.0.0\n  4146→\n  4147→    interpolation : str, optional\n  4148→        Deprecated name for the method keyword argument.\n  4149→\n  4150→        .. deprecated:: 1.22.0\n  4151→\n  4152→    Returns\n  4153→    -------\n  4154→    percentile : scalar or ndarray\n  4155→        If `q` is a single percentile and `axis=None`, then the result\n  4156→        is a scalar. If multiple percentiles are given, first axis of\n  4157→        the result corresponds to the percentiles. The other axes are\n  4158→        the axes that remain after the reduction of `a`. If the input\n  4159→        contains integers or floats smaller than ``float64``, the output\n  4160→        data-type is ``float64``. Otherwise, the output data-type is the\n  4161→        same as that of the input. If `out` is specified, that array is\n  4162→        returned instead.\n  4163→\n  4164→    See Also\n  4165→    --------\n  4166→    mean\n  4167→    median : equivalent to ``percentile(..., 50)``\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. Default is 'valid'.\n  5852→\n  5853→        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n  5854→          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n  5855→        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n  5856→          output is the same size as the larger input.\n  5857→        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n  5858→          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n  5859→\n  5860→    Returns\n  5861→    -------\n  5862→    rolling_med : ndarray\n  5863→        The rolling median of the input array. Must be a positive integer.\n  5848→    axis : int, optional\n  5849→        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n  5850→    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n  5851→        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n  5852→\n  5853→        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n  5854→          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n  5855→        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n  5856→          output is the same size as the larger input.\n  5857→        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n  5858→          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n  5859→\n  5860→    Returns\n  5861→    -------\n  5862→    rolling_med : ndarray\n  5863→        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n  5864→        parameter and the input shape.\n  5865→\n  5866→    Examples\n  5867→    --------\n  5868→    >>> import numpy as np\n  5869→    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n  5870→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n  5871→    array([2., 3., 4.])\n  5872→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n  5873→    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n  5874→    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n  5875→    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n  5876→\n  5877→    Notes\n  5878→    -----\n  5879→    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n  5880→    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n  5881→    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n  5882→    array is padded on both sides.\n  5883→\n  5884→    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n  5885→    each window.\n  5886→    \"\"\"\n  5887→    a = asanyarray(a)\n  5888→\n  5889→    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n  5890→        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n  5891→\n  5892→    window_size = int(window_size)\n  5893→    if window_size <= 0:\n  5894→        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n  5895→\n  5896→    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n  5897→        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n  5898→\n  5899→    # Normalize axis\n  5900→    if axis is None:\n  5901→        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n  5902→\n  5903→    ndim = a.ndim\n  5904→    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n  5905→\n  5906→    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n  5907→        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - Expected gains: 2-3x speedup for large arrays, 50-75% memory reduction.\"\n}\n```\n\n## Summary\n\nI've completed a thorough efficiency analysis of the `rolling_median` function in `/workspace/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py` (lines 5828-5974). The review identified **5 major efficiency issues**:\n\n### Critical Issues (HIGH impact):\n1. **Lines 5946-5947 - Unnecessary padding copy**: `np.pad()` materializes the entire padded array instead of using views, creating 1-2x memory overhead\n2. **Lines 5963-5965 - Hot-path loop inefficiency**: Python loop calling `np.median()` repeatedly yields O(n*w*log(w)) complexity instead of O(n), causing 100K+ median calls for reasonable array sizes\n3. **Lines 5951-5957 - No stride tricks**: Missing `np.lib.stride_tricks.as_strided()` for windowing, forces iteration instead of vectorized batch computation\n\n### Medium Impact:\n4. **Lines 5951-5957 - Redundant moveaxis**: May create additional copy if output isn't contiguous\n\n### Low Impact:\n5. **Line 5960 - Wasted zero-fill**: `np.zeros()` initializes buffer that's immediately overwritten; should use `np.empty()`\n\nThe recommended fix combines padding, axis movement, and windowing into a single `as_strided()` call, eliminating copies and enabling either vectorized median or an O(n) sliding median algorithm with appropriate data structures."}],"totalDurationMs":32124,"totalTokens":18838,"totalToolUseCount":8,"usage":{"input_tokens":4,"cache_creation_input_tokens":2276,"cache_read_input_tokens":15407,"output_tokens":1151,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":2276,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}}}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01KrEmR11iQ9QiRnW7e3xXUf","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01Hk5vK8Z7BB4YfoTe1DBF7F","name":"Edit","input":{"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py","old_string":"def _rolling_median_dispatcher(a, window_size, axis=None, mode=None):\n    \"\"\"Dispatcher for rolling_median.\"\"\"\n    return (a,)\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_rolling_median_dispatcher)\ndef rolling_median(a, window_size, *, axis=-1, mode='valid'):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the rolling median of a 1-D or N-D array.\n\n    For each position in the array, compute the median of values in a window\n    of specified size centered (or left-aligned) around that position.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    a : array_like\n        Input array or object that can be converted to an array.\n    window_size : int\n        Size of the rolling window. Must be a positive integer.\n    axis : int, optional\n        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n\n        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n          output is the same size as the larger input.\n        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    rolling_med : ndarray\n        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n        parameter and the input shape.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n    array([2., 3., 4.])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n    array is padded on both sides.\n\n    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n    each window.\n    \"\"\"\n    a = asanyarray(a)\n\n    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n\n    window_size = int(window_size)\n    if window_size <= 0:\n        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n\n    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n\n    # Normalize axis\n    if axis is None:\n        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n\n    ndim = a.ndim\n    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n\n    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - 1\n            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n\n    # Compute padding\n    if mode == 'valid':\n        pad_before = 0\n        pad_after = 0\n        output_size = axis_size - 1\n        pad_after = window_size - 1\n        output_size = axis_size + window_size - 1\n\n    # Apply padding\n    padded_shape = list(a.shape)\n    padded_shape[axis] = axis_size + pad_before + pad_after\n    padded = np.pad(a, [(pad_before, pad_after) if i == axis else (0, 0)\n                        for i in range(ndim)], mode='edge')\n\n    # Compute rolling median using a loop over windows\n    # Move axis to position -1 for easier iteration\n    a_moved = np.moveaxis(padded, axis, -1)\n    original_shape = a_moved.shape\n\n    # Reshape to (batch, length) for easier processing\n    batch_shape = original_shape[:-1]\n    length = original_shape[-1]\n    a_flat = a_moved.reshape(-1, length)\n\n    # Initialize output\n    output_flat = np.zeros((a_flat.shape[0], output_size), dtype=np.float64)\n\n    # Compute rolling median for each batch element\n    for i in range(output_size):\n        window = a_flat[:, i:i + window_size]\n        output_flat[:, i] = np.median(window, axis=1)\n\n    # Reshape back\n    output_shape = list(batch_shape) + [output_size]\n    output = output_flat.reshape(output_shape)\n\n    # Move axis back to original position\n    output = np.moveaxis(output, -1, axis)\n\n    return output","new_string":"def _rolling_median_dispatcher(a, window_size, axis=None, mode=None):\n    \"\"\"Dispatcher for rolling_median.\"\"\"\n    return (a,)\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_rolling_median_dispatcher)\ndef rolling_median(a, window_size, *, axis=-1, mode='valid'):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the rolling median of a 1-D or N-D array.\n\n    For each position in the array, compute the median of values in a window\n    of specified size centered (or left-aligned) around that position.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    a : array_like\n        Input array or object that can be converted to an array.\n    window_size : int\n        Size of the rolling window. If `a` is not an array, a\n        conversion is attempted.\n    axis : None or int or tuple of ints, optional\n        Axis or axes along which to average `a`.  The default,\n        `axis=None`, will average over all of the elements of the input array.\n        If axis is negative it counts from the last to the first axis.\n        If axis is a tuple of ints, averaging is performed on all of the axes\n        specified in the tuple instead of a single axis or all the axes as\n        before.\n    weights : array_like, optional\n        An array of weights associated with the values in `a`. Each value in\n        `a` contributes to the average according to its associated weight.\n        The array of weights must be the same shape as `a` if no axis is\n        specified, otherwise the weights must have dimensions and shape\n        consistent with `a` along the specified axis.\n        If `weights=None`, then all data in `a` are assumed to have a\n        weight equal to one.\n        The calculation is::\n\n            avg = sum(a * weights) / sum(weights)\n\n        where the sum is over all included elements.\n        The only constraint on the values of `weights` is that `sum(weights)`\n        must not be 0.\n    returned : bool, optional\n        Default is `False`. If `True`, the tuple (`average`, `sum_of_weights`)\n        is returned, otherwise only the average is returned.\n        If `weights=None`, `sum_of_weights` is equivalent to the number of\n        elements over which the average is taken.\n    keepdims : bool, optional\n        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left\n        in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,\n        the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.\n        *Note:* `keepdims` will not work with instances of `numpy.matrix`\n        or other classes whose methods do not support `keepdims`.\n\n        .. versionadded:: 1.23.0\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    retval, [sum_of_weights] : array_type or double\n        Return the average along the specified axis. When `returned` is `True`,\n        return a tuple with the average as the first element and the sum\n        of the weights as the second element. `sum_of_weights` is of the\n        same type as `retval`. The result dtype follows a general pattern.\n        If `weights` is None, the result dtype will be that of `a` , or ``float64``\n        if `a` is integral. Otherwise, if `weights` is not None and `a` is non-\n        integral, the result type will be the type of lowest precision capable of\n        representing values of both `a` and `weights`. If `a` happens to be\n        integral, the previous rules still applies but the result dtype will\n        at least be ``float64``.\n\n    Raises\n    ------\n    ZeroDivisionError\n        When all weights along axis are zero. See `numpy.ma.average` for a\n        version robust to this type of error.\n    TypeError\n        When `weights` does not have the same shape as `a`, and `axis=None`.\n    ValueError\n        When `weights` does not have dimensions and shape consistent with `a`\n        along specified `axis`.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    mean\n\n    ma.average : average for masked arrays -- useful if your data contains\n                 \"missing\" values\n    numpy.result_type : Returns the type that results from applying the\n                        numpy type promotion rules to the arguments.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> data = np.arange(1, 5)\n    >>> data\n    array([1, 2, 3, 4])\n    >>> np.average(data)\n    2.5\n    >>> np.average(np.arange(1, 11), weights=np.arange(10, 0, -1))\n    4.0\n\n    >>> data = np.arange(6).reshape((3, 2))\n    >>> data\n    array([[0, 1],\n           [2, 3],\n           [4, 5]])\n    >>> array, checking for NaNs or Infs.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    a : array_like\n        Input data, in any form that can be converted to an array.  This\n        includes lists, lists of tuples, tuples, tuples of tuples, tuples\n        of lists and ndarrays.  Success requires no NaNs or Infs.\n    dtype : data-type, optional\n        By default, the data-type is inferred from the input data.\n    order : {'C', 'F', 'A', 'K'}, optional\n        Memory layout.  'A' and 'K' depend on the order of input array a.\n        'C' row-major (C-style),\n        'F' column-major (Fortran-style) memory representation.\n        'A' (any) means 'F' if `a` is Fortran contiguous, 'C' otherwise\n        'K' (keep) preserve input order\n        Defaults to 'C'.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray\n        Array interpretation of `a`.  No copy is performed if the input\n        is already an ndarray.  If `a` is a subclass of ndarray, a base\n        class ndarray is returned.\n\n    Raises\n    ------\n    ValueError\n        Raises ValueError if `a` contains NaN (Not a Number) or Inf (Infinity).\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    asarray : Create and array.\n    asanyarray : Similar function which passes through subclasses.\n    ascontiguousarray : Convert input to a contiguous array.\n    asfortranarray : Convert input to an ndarray with column-major\n                     memory order.\n    fromiter : Create an array from an iterator.\n    fromfunction : Construct an array by executing a function on grid\n                   positions.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    Convert a list into an array. If all elements are finite, then\n    ``asarray_chkfinite`` is identical to ``asarray``.\n\n    >>> a = [1, 2]\n    >>> np.asarray_chkfinite(a, dtype=float)\n    array([1., 2.])\n\n    Raises ValueError if array_like contains Nans or Infs.\n\n    >>> a = [1, 2, np.inf]\n    >>> try:\n    ...     np.asarray_chkfinite(a)\n    ... except ValueError:\n    ...     print('ValueError')\n    ...\n    ValueError\n\n    \"\"\"\n    a = asarray(a, dtype=dtype, order=order)\n    if a.dtype.char in typecodes['AllFloat'] and not np.isfinite(a).all():\n        raise ValueError(\n            \"array must not contain infs or NaNs\")\n    return a\n\n\ndef _piecewise_dispatcher(x, condlist, funclist, *args, **kw):\n    yield x\n    # support the undocumented behavior of allowing scalars\n    if np.iterable(condlist):\n        yield from condlist\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_piecewise_dispatcher)\ndef piecewise(x, condlist, funclist, *args, **kw):\n    \"\"\"\n    Evaluate a piecewise-defined function.\n\n    Given a set of conditions and corresponding functions, evaluate each\n    function on the input data wherever its condition is true.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : ndarray or scalar\n        The input domain.\n    condlist : list of bool arrays or bool scalars\n        Each boolean array corresponds to a function in `funclist`.  Wherever\n        `condlist[i]` is True, `funclist[i](x)` is used as the output value.\n\n        Each boolean array in `condlist` selects a piece of `x`,\n        and should therefore be of the same shape as `x`.\n\n        The length of `condlist` must correspond to that of `funclist`.\n        If one extra function is given, i.e. if\n        ``len(funclist) == len(condlist) + 1``, then that extra function\n        is the default value, used wherever all conditions are false.\n    funclist : list of callables, f(x,*args,**kw), or scalars\n        Each function is evaluated over `x` wherever its corresponding\n        condition is True.  It should take a 1d array as input and give an 1d\n        array or a scalar value as output.  If, instead of a callable,\n        a scalar is provided then a constant function (``lambda x: scalar``) is\n        assumed.\n    args : tuple, optional\n        Any further arguments given to `piecewise` are passed to the functions\n        upon execution, i.e., if called ``piecewise(..., ..., 1, 'a')``, then\n        each function is called as ``f(x, 1, 'a')``.\n    kw : dict, optional\n        Keyword arguments used in calling `piecewise` are passed to the\n        functions upon execution, i.e., if called\n        ``piecewise(..., ..., alpha=1)``, then each function is called as\n        ``f(x, alpha=1)``.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray\n        The output is the same shape and type as x and is found by\n        calling the functions in `funclist` on the appropriate portions of `x`,\n        as defined by the boolean arrays in `condlist`.  Portions not covered\n        by any condition have a default value of 0.\n\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    choose, select, where\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    This is similar to choose or select, except that functions are\n    evaluated on elements of `x` that satisfy the corresponding condition from\n    `condlist`.\n\n    The result is::\n\n            |--\n            |funclist[0](x[condlist[0]])\n      out = |funclist[1](x[condlist[1]])\n            |...\n            |funclist[n2](x[condlist[n2]])\n            |--\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    Define the signum function, which is -1 for ``x < 0`` and +1 for ``x >= 0``.\n\n    >>> x = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 6)\n    >>> np.piecewise(x, [x < 0, x >= 0], [-1, 1])\n    array([-1., -1., -1.,  1.,  1.,  1.])\n\n    Define the absolute value, which is ``-x`` for ``x <0`` and ``x`` for\n    ``x >= 0``.\n\n    >>> np.piecewise(x, [x < 0, x >= 0], [lambda x: -x, lambda x: x])\n    array([2.5,  1.5,  0.5,  0.5,  1.5,  2.5])\n\n    Apply the same function to a scalar value.\n\n    >>> y = -2\n    >>> np.piecewise(y, [y < 0, y >= 0], [lambda x: -x, lambda x: x])\n    array(2)\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = asanyarray(x)\n    n2 = len(funclist)\n\n    # undocumented: single condition is promoted to a list of one condition\n    if isscalar(condlist) or (\n            not isinstance(condlist[0], (list, ndarray)) and x.ndim != 0):\n        condlist = [condlist]\n\n    condlist = asarray(condlist, dtype=bool)\n    n = len(condlist)\n\n    if n == n2 - 1:  # compute the \"otherwise\" condition.\n        condelse = ~np.any(condlist, axis=0, keepdims=True)\n        condlist = np.concatenate([condlist, condelse], axis=0)\n        n += 1\n    elif n != n2:\n        raise ValueError(\n            \"with {} condition(s), either {} or {} functions are expected\"\n            .format(n, n, n+1)\n        )\n\n    y = zeros_like(x)\n    for cond, func in zip(condlist, funclist):\n        if not isinstance(func, collections.abc.Callable):\n            y[cond] = func\n        else:\n            vals = x[cond]\n            if vals.size > 0:\n                y[cond] = func(vals, *args, **kw)\n\n    return y\n\n\ndef _select_dispatcher(condlist, choicelist, default=None):\n    yield from condlist\n    yield from choicelist\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_select_dispatcher)\ndef select(condlist, choicelist, default=0):\n    \"\"\"\n    Return an array drawn from elements in choicelist, depending on conditions.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    condlist : list of bool ndarrays\n        The list of conditions which determine from which array in `choicelist`\n        the output elements are taken. When multiple conditions are satisfied,\n        the first one encountered in `condlist` is used.\n    choicelist : list of ndarrays\n        The list of arrays from which the output elements are taken. It has\n        to be of the same length as `condlist`.\n    default : scalar, optional\n        The element inserted in `output` when all conditions evaluate to False.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    output : ndarray\n        The output at position m is the m-th element of the array in\n        `choicelist` where the m-th element of the corresponding array in\n        `condlist` is True.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    where : Return elements from one of two arrays depending on condition.\n    take, choose, compress, diag, diagonal\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    Beginning with an array of integers from 0 to 5 (inclusive),\n    elements less than ``3`` are negated, elements greater than ``3``\n    are squared, and elements not meeting either of these conditions\n    (exactly ``3``) are replaced with a `default` value of ``42``.\n\n    >>> x = np.arange(6)\n    >>> condlist = [x<3, x>3]\n    >>> choicelist = [x, x**2]\n    >>> np.select(condlist, choicelist, 42)\n    array([ 0,  1,  2, 42, 16, 25])\n\n    When multiple conditions are satisfied, the first one encountered in\n    `condlist` is used.\n\n    >>> condlist = [x<=4, x>3]\n    >>> choicelist = [x, x**2]\n    >>> np.select(condlist, choicelist, 55)\n    array([ 0,  1,  2,  3,  4, 25])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    # Check the size of condlist and choicelist are the same, or abort.\n    if len(condlist) != len(choicelist):\n        raise ValueError(\n            'list of cases must be same length as list of conditions')\n\n    # Now that the dtype is known, handle the deprecated select([], []) case\n    if len(condlist) == 0:\n        raise ValueError(\"select with an empty condition list is not possible\")\n\n    # TODO: This preserves the Python int, float, complex manually to get the\n    #       right `result_type` with NEP 50.  Most likely we will grow a better\n    #       way to spell this (and this can be replaced).\n    choicelist = [\n        choice if type(choice) in (int, float, complex) else np.asarray(choice)\n        for choice in choicelist]\n    choicelist.append(default if type(default) in (int, float, complex)\n                      else np.asarray(default))\n\n    try:\n        dtype = np.result_type(*choicelist)\n    except TypeError as e:\n        msg = f'Choicelist and default value do not have a common dtype: {e}'\n        raise TypeError(msg) from None\n\n    # Convert conditions to arrays and broadcast conditions and choices\n    # as the shape is needed for the result. Doing it separately optimizes\n    # for example when all choices are scalars.\n    condlist = np.broadcast_arrays(*condlist)\n    choicelist = np.broadcast_arrays(*choicelist)\n\n    # If cond array is not an ndarray in boolean format or scalar bool, abort.\n    for i, cond in enumerate(condlist):\n        if cond.dtype.type is not np.bool:\n            raise TypeError(\n                'invalid entry {} in condlist: should be boolean ndarray'.format(i))\n\n    if choicelist[0].ndim == 0:\n        # This may be common, so avoid the call.\n        result_shape = condlist[0].shape\n    else:\n        result_shape = np.broadcast_arrays(condlist[0], choicelist[0])[0].shape\n\n    result = np.full(result_shape, choicelist[-1], dtype)\n\n    # Use np.copyto to burn each choicelist array onto result, using the\n    # corresponding condlist as a boolean mask. This is done in reverse\n    # order since the first choice should take precedence.\n    choicelist = choicelist[-2::-1]\n    condlist = condlist[::-1]\n    for choice, cond in zip(choicelist, condlist):\n        np.copyto(result, choice, where=cond)\n\n    return result\n\n\ndef _copy_dispatcher(a, order=None, subok=None):\n    return (a,)\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_copy_dispatcher)\ndef copy(a, order='K', subok=False):\n    \"\"\"\n    Return an array copy of the given object.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    a : array_like\n        Input data.\n    order : {'C', 'F', 'A', 'K'}, optional\n        Controls the memory layout of the copy. 'C' means C-order,\n        'F' means F-order, 'A' means 'F' if `a` is Fortran contiguous,\n        'C' otherwise. 'K' means match the layout of `a` as closely\n        as possible. (Note that this function and :meth:`ndarray.copy` are very\n        similar, but have different default values for their order=\n        arguments.)\n    subok : bool, optional\n        If True, then sub-classes will be passed-through, otherwise the\n        returned array will be forced to be a base-class array (defaults to False).\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    arr : ndarray\n        Array interpretation of `a`.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    ndarray.copy : Preferred method for creating an array copy\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    This is equivalent to:\n\n    >>> np.array(a, copy=True)  #doctest: +SKIP\n\n    The copy made of the data is shallow, i.e., for arrays with object dtype,\n    the new array will point to the same objects.\n    See Examples from `ndarray.copy`.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    Create an array x, with a reference y and a copy z:\n\n    >>> x = np.array([1, 2, 3])\n    >>> y = x\n    >>> z = np.copy(x)\n\n    Note that, when we modify x, y changes, but not z:\n\n    >>> x[0] = 10\n    >>> x[0] == y[0]\n    True\n    >>> x[0] == z[0]\n    False\n\n    Note that, np.copy clears previously set WRITEABLE=False flag.\n\n    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3])\n    >>> a.flags[\"WRITEABLE\"] = False\n    >>> b = np.copy(a)\n    >>> b.flags[\"WRITEABLE\"]\n    True\n    >>> b[0] = 3\n    >>> b\n    array([3, 2, 3])\n    \"\"\"\n    return array(a, order=order, subok=subok, copy=True)\n\n# Basic operations\n\n\ndef _gradient_dispatcher(f, *varargs, axis=None, edge_order=None):\n    yield f\n    yield from varargs\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_gradient_dispatcher)\ndef gradient(f, *varargs, axis=None, edge_order=1):\n    \"\"\"\n    Return the gradient of an N-dimensional array.\n\n    The gradient is computed using second order accurate central differences\n    in the interior points and either first or second order accurate one-sides\n    (forward or backwards) differences at the boundaries.\n    The returned gradient hence has the same shape as the input array.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    f : array_like\n        An N-dimensional array containing samples of a scalar function.\n    varargs : list of scalar or array, optional\n        Spacing between f values. Default unitary spacing for all dimensions.\n        Spacing can be specified using:\n\n        1. single scalar to specify a sample distance for all dimensions.\n        2. N scalars to specify a constant sample distance for each dimension.\n           i.e. `dx`, `dy`, `dz`, ...\n        3.
