bins)\n\n\n@array_function_dispatch(_digitize_dispatcher)\ndef digitize(x, bins, right=False):\n    \"\"\"\n    Return the indices of the bins to which each value in input array belongs.\n\n    =========  =============  ============================\n    `right`    order of bins  returned index `i` satisfies\n    =========  =============  ============================\n    ``False``  increasing     ``bins[i-1] <= x < bins[i]``\n    ``True``   increasing     ``bins[i-1] < x <= bins[i]``\n    ``False``  decreasing     ``bins[i-1] > x >= bins[i]``\n    ``True``   decreasing     ``bins[i-1] >= x > bins[i]``\n    =========  =============  ============================\n\n    If values in `x` are beyond the bounds of `bins`, 0 or ``len(bins)`` is\n    returned as appropriate.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n        Input array to be binned. Prior to NumPy 1.10.0, this array had to\n        be 1-dimensional, but can now have any shape.\n    bins : array_like\n        Array of bins. It has to be 1-dimensional and monotonic.\n    right : bool, optional\n        Indicating whether the intervals include the right or the left bin\n        edge. Default behavior is (right==False) indicating that the interval\n        does not include the right edge. The left bin end is open in this\n        case, i.e., bins[i-1] <= x < bins[i] is the default behavior for\n        monotonically increasing bins.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    indices : ndarray of ints\n        Output array of indices, of same shape as `x`.\n\n    Raises\n    ------\n    ValueError\n        If `bins` is not monotonic.\n    TypeError\n        If the type of the input is complex.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    bincount, histogram, unique, searchsorted\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    If values in `x` are such that they fall outside the bin range,\n    attempting to index `bins` with the indices that `digitize` returns\n    will result in an IndexError.\n\n    .. versionadded:: 1.10.0\n\n    `numpy.digitize` is  implemented in terms of `numpy.searchsorted`.\n    This means that a binary search is used to bin the values, which scales\n    much better for larger number of bins than the previous linear search.\n    It also removes the requirement for the input array to be 1-dimensional.\n\n    For monotonically *increasing* `bins`, the following are equivalent::\n\n        np.digitize(x, bins, right=True)\n        np.searchsorted(bins, x, side='left')\n\n    Note that as the order of the arguments are reversed, the side must be too.\n    The `searchsorted` call is marginally faster, as it does not do any\n    monotonicity checks. Perhaps more importantly, it supports all dtypes.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> x = np.array([0.2, 6.4, 3.0, 1.6])\n    >>> bins = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 2.5, 4.0, 10.0])\n    >>> inds = np.digitize(x, bins)\n    >>> inds\n    array([1, 4, 3, 2])\n    >>> for n in range(x.size):\n    ...   print(bins[inds[n]-1], \"<=\", x[n], \"<\", bins[inds[n]])\n    ...\n    0.0 <= 0.2 < 1.0\n    4.0 <= 6.4 < 10.0\n    2.5 <= 3.0 < 4.0\n    1.0 <= 1.6 < 2.5\n\n    >>> x = np.array([1.2, 10.0, 12.4, 15.5, 20.])\n    >>> bins = np.array([0, 5, 10, 15, 20])\n    >>> np.digitize(x,bins,right=True)\n    array([1, 2, 3, 4, 4])\n    >>> np.digitize(x,bins,right=False)\n    array([1, 3, 3, 4, 5])\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _nx.asarray(x)\n    bins = _nx.asarray(bins)\n\n    # here for compatibility, searchsorted below is happy to take this\n    if np.issubdtype(x.dtype, _nx.complexfloating):\n        raise TypeError(\"x may not be complex\")\n\n    mono = _monotonicity(bins)\n    if mono == 0:\n        raise ValueError(\"bins must be monotonically increasing or decreasing\")\n\n    # this is backwards because the arguments below are swapped\n    side = 'left' if right else 'right'\n    if mono == -1:\n        # reverse the bins, and invert the results\n        return len(bins) - Must be a positive integer.\n    axis : int, optional\n        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n\n        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n          output is the same size as the larger input.\n        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    rolling_med : ndarray\n        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n        parameter and the input shape.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n    array([2., 3., 4.])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n    array is padded on both sides.\n\n    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n    each window.\n    \"\"\"\n    a = asanyarray(a)\n\n    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n\n    window_size = int(window_size)\n    if window_size <= 0:\n        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n\n    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n\n    # Normalize axis\n    if axis is None:\n        raise ValueError(\"axis cannot be None for rolling_median\")\n\n    ndim = a.ndim\n    axis = axis if axis >= 0 else ndim + axis\n\n    if axis < 0 or axis >= ndim:\n        raise ValueError(f\"axis {axis - ndim if axis >= 0 else axis} is out of bounds for array of dimension {ndim}\")\n\n    # Get the size along the specified axis\n    axis_size = a.shape[axis]\n\n    # Handle edge cases\n    if window_size > axis_size:\n        # Return NaN for entire output if window is larger than array\n        if mode == 'valid':\n            # No valid output\n            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n            new_shape[axis] = 0\n            return empty(tuple(new_shape), dtype=np.float64)\n        elif mode == 'same':\n            return np.full(a.shape, np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n        else:  # mode == 'full'\n            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n            new_shape[axis] = axis_size + window_size - 1\n            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n\n    # Compute padding\n    if mode == 'valid':\n        pad_before = 0\n        pad_after = 0\n        output_size = axis_size - window_size + 1\n    elif mode == 'same':\n        # Pad to maintain same size\n        total_pad = window_size - 1\n        pad_before = total_pad // 2\n        pad_after = total_pad - pad_before\n        output_size = axis_size\n    else:  # mode == 'full'\n        pad_before = window_size - 1\n        pad_after = window_size - 1\n        output_size = axis_size + window_size - 1\n\n    # Apply padding\n    padded_shape = list(a.shape)\n    padded_shape[axis] = axis_size + pad_before + pad_after\n    padded = np.pad(a, [(pad_before, pad_after) if i == axis else (0, 0)\n                        for i in range(ndim)], mode='edge')\n\n    # Initialize output\n    output_flat = np.zeros((a_flat.shape[0], output_size), dtype=np.float64)\n\n    # Compute rolling median for each batch element\n    for i in range(output_size):\n        window = a_flat[:, i:i + window_size]\n        output_flat[:, i] = np.median(window, axis=1)\n\n    # Reshape back\n    output_shape = list(batch_shape) + [output_size]\n    output = output_flat.reshape(output_shape)\n\n    # Move axis back to original position\n    output = np.moveaxis(output, -1, axis)\n\n    return output\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":5884,"oldLines":6,"newStart":5884,"newLines":8,"lines":["     This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of","     each window.","     \"\"\"","+    from numpy.lib._stride_tricks_impl import sliding_window_view","+","     a = asanyarray(a)"," ","     if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):"]},{"oldStart":5897,"oldLines":23,"newStart":5899,"newLines":15,"lines":["         raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")"," ","     # Must be a positive integer.\n    axis : int, optional\n        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n\n        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n          output is the same size as the larger input.\n        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    rolling_med : ndarray\n        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n        parameter and the input shape.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n    array([2., 3., 4.])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. Must be a positive integer.\n+    axis : int, optional\n+        The axis along which to compute the rolling median. Default is -1 (last axis).\n+    mode : {'valid', 'same', 'full'}, optional\n+        Determines the output size. Default is 'valid'.\n+\n+        - 'valid': Output size is ``max(M, N) - min(M, N) + 1``.\n+          Computes output only where the window fully overlaps with the input.\n+        - 'same': Output size is ``max(M, N)``. Pads input on one end so that\n+          output is the same size as the larger input.\n+        - 'full': Output size is ``M + N - 1``. Pads input so output includes\n+          effects of the window at every point of overlap.\n+\n+    Returns\n+    -------\n+    rolling_med : ndarray\n+        The rolling median of the input array. The shape depends on the `mode`\n+        parameter and the input shape.\n+\n+    Examples\n+    --------\n+    >>> import numpy as np\n+    >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3)\n+    array([2., 3., 4.])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='same')\n+    array([1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5])\n+    >>> np.rolling_median(a, 3, mode='full')\n+    array([1. , 1.5, 2. , 3. , 4. , 4.5, 5. ])\n+\n+    Notes\n+    -----\n+    The rolling median is computed using a sliding window approach. For\n+    'valid' mode, the window is fully contained within the array. For\n+    'same' mode, the array is padded symmetrically. For 'full' mode, the\n+    array is padded on both sides.\n+\n+    This function uses numpy's median function to compute the median of\n+    each window.\n+    \"\"\"\n+    from numpy.lib._stride_tricks_impl import sliding_window_view\n+\n+    a = asanyarray(a)\n+\n+    if not isinstance(window_size, (int, np.integer)):\n+        raise TypeError(\"window_size must be an integer\")\n+\n+    window_size = int(window_size)\n+    if window_size <= 0:\n+        raise ValueError(\"window_size must be a positive integer\")\n+\n+    if mode not in ('valid', 'same', 'full'):\n+        raise ValueError(f\"mode must be 'valid', 'same', or 'full', got '{mode}'\")\n+\n+    # Normalize axis\n+    ndim = a.ndim\n+    axis = normalize_axis_index(axis, ndim)\n+\n+    # Get the size along the specified axis\n+    axis_size = a.shape[axis]\n+\n+    # Handle edge cases\n+    if window_size > axis_size:\n+        if mode == 'valid':\n+            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n+            new_shape[axis] = 0\n+            return empty(tuple(new_shape), dtype=np.float64)\n+        elif mode == 'same':\n+            return np.full(a.shape, np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n+        else:  # mode == 'full'\n+            new_shape = list(a.shape)\n+            new_shape[axis] = axis_size + window_size - 1\n+            return np.full(tuple(new_shape), np.nan, dtype=np.float64)\n+\n+    # Determine padding\n+    pad_dict = {\n+        'valid': (0, 0),\n+        'same': ((window_size - 1) // 2, (window_size - 1) - (window_size - 1) // 2),\n+        'full': (window_size - 1, window_size - 1)\n+    }\n+    pad_before, pad_after = pad_dict[mode]\n+\n+    # Apply padding if needed\n+    if pad_before > 0 or pad_after > 0:\n+        padded = np.pad(a, [(pad_before, pad_after) if i == axis else (0, 0)\n+                            for i in range(ndim)], mode='edge')\n+    else:\n+        padded = a\n+\n+    # Create sliding window view and compute median\n+    windowed = sliding_window_view(padded, window_size, axis=axis)\n+    return np.median(windowed, axis=-1)","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"d4cb46fc-49fd-49ab-9b24-1539e74def50","uuid":"2e3129d4-3672-4349-8777-7f1630e51ce2","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"diff --git a/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py b/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py\nindex 3fa9c5f..5b872fc 100644\n--- a/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py\n+++ b/numpy/lib/_function_base_impl.py\n@@ -41,7 +41,7 @@\n     'diff', 'gradient', 'angle', 'unwrap', 'sort_complex', 'flip',\n     'rot90', 'extract', 'place', 'vectorize', 'asarray_chkfinite', 'average',\n     'bincount', 'digitize', 'cov', 'corrcoef',\n-    'median', 'sinc', 'hamming', 'hanning', 'bartlett',\n+    'median', 'rolling_median', 'sinc', 'hamming', 'hanning', 'bartlett',\n     'blackman', 'kaiser', 'trapezoid', 'trapz', 'i0',\n     'meshgrid', 'delete', 'insert', 'append', 'interp',\n     'quantile'\n@@ -5825,3 +5825,114 @@ def digitize(x, bins, right=False):\n         return len(bins) -
